label: ai
topic: Review of a horror film about sinister imaginary friends that lacks imagination
text:
There is an undeniable, primal allure to the corruption of childhood innocence in horror cinema. The premise of an imaginary friend turning out to be a sinister, malevolent entity is practically a goldmine for psychological terror. Unfortunately, the latest entry into this subgenre squanders its compelling conceptual foundation through a disastrous combination of poor worldbuilding, agonizingly clunky exposition, and an overwhelming reliance on tropes lifted entirely from better films. Walking into the campus cinema last weekend with my roommates, I was genuinely excited, hoping for a nuanced exploration of childhood trauma. Instead, I found myself repeatedly checking my watch, waiting for the runtime to mercifully end.

The film's greatest offense is its staggering inability to trust the audience. Rather than allowing the horror to unfold organically through atmosphere or visual storytelling, the screenplay grinds to a halt every twenty minutes to have characters explain the lore out loud. In one particularly egregious scene, a supposedly eccentric paranormal historian literally maps out the rules of the "Never-Ever" realm on a chalkboard, stripping away any sense of mystery. This is worldbuilding at its most mechanical, tedious, and condescending. We are told, rather than shown, why this alternate dimension is terrifying, which completely deflates any lingering tension.

Furthermore, it is impossible to ignore how heavily the film leans on the visual and narrative frameworks of other, vastly superior works. The climax, which features our protagonist navigating a surreal, Escher-esque labyrinth of doors to rescue her stepdaughter, feels like a direct, uninspired copy of the "Further" from James Wan's filmography—specifically Insidious—mixed with a very familiar button-eyed aesthetic. However, lacking the meticulous craftsmanship of its influences, these sequences feel like a cheap haunted house attraction rather than a descent into a psychological nightmare. I caught myself rolling my eyes when the monstrous true form of the entity was finally revealed, looking more like a discarded theme park animatronic than a manifestation of childhood grief.

If there is a single redeeming quality to be salvaged from this otherwise derivative mess, it is the astonishing performance by the young child lead. Amidst a cast of adults who seem to be sleepwalking through their thinly written roles, she delivers a turn of genuine, unnerving brilliance. There is a specific scene early in the second act where she is having a seemingly innocent tea party with the empty space across from her. The camera slowly zooms in as her cheerful demeanor shifts infinitesimally into a look of absolute, paralyzed dread. She barely raises her voice, but the sheer terror in her eyes practically leaps off the screen. For those three uninterrupted minutes, I was genuinely captivated, feeling the exact kind of visceral dread the rest of the film failed to replicate.

Ultimately, one extraordinary child performance is simply not enough to anchor a film that is fundamentally broken at the script level. The movie attempts to weave a poignant narrative about the lingering scars of childhood trauma, but buries its own thematic weight under a mountain of cheap jump scares and borrowed aesthetics. As a horror fan, there is nothing quite as frustrating as watching a genuinely brilliant premise wither away due to sheer creative laziness. I left the theater not with a lingering sense of unease, but with a profound sense of wasted potential. It is a film that will likely be forgotten by the time the semester ends, serving only as a textbook example of how not to write a screenplay.
